content:
# Matthew Chapter 1 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus (א), 4th century CE, Quire A, first folio of Matthew | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/MAT_001_page_001.jpg, MAT_001_page_002.jpg

### The Lineage of Jesus the Messiah

1. This is the record of the origin of Jesus the Messiah — son of David, son of Abraham.

2. Abraham fathered Isaac. Isaac fathered Jacob. Jacob fathered Judah and his brothers.

3. Judah fathered Perez and Zerah by Tamar. Perez fathered Hezron. Hezron fathered Ram.

4. Ram fathered Amminadab. Amminadab fathered Nahshon. Nahshon fathered Salmon.

5. Salmon fathered Boaz by Rahab. Boaz fathered Obed by Ruth. Obed fathered Jesse.

6. And Jesse fathered David the king.

David fathered Solomon by the wife of Uriah.

7. Solomon fathered Rehoboam. Rehoboam fathered Abijah. Abijah fathered Asaph.

8. Asaph fathered Jehoshaphat. Jehoshaphat fathered Joram. Joram fathered Uzziah.

9. Uzziah fathered Jotham. Jotham fathered Ahaz. Ahaz fathered Hezekiah.

10. Hezekiah fathered Manasseh. Manasseh fathered Amos. Amos fathered Josiah.

11. And Josiah fathered Jechoniah and his brothers, at the time of the exile to Babylon.

12. After the exile to Babylon: Jechoniah fathered Shealtiel. Shealtiel fathered Zerubbabel.

13. Zerubbabel fathered Abiud. Abiud fathered Eliakim. Eliakim fathered Azor.

14. Azor fathered Zadok. Zadok fathered Achim. Achim fathered Eliud.

15. Eliud fathered Eleazar. Eleazar fathered Matthan. Matthan fathered Jacob.

16. And Jacob fathered Joseph, the husband of Mary — and from her was born Jesus, who is called the Messiah.

17. So then: from Abraham to David, fourteen generations. From David to the exile in Babylon, fourteen generations. And from the exile in Babylon to the Messiah, fourteen generations.

---

### The Birth of Jesus the Messiah

18. Now the birth of Jesus the Messiah happened like this. His mother Mary was pledged in marriage to Joseph, but before they came together, she was found to be carrying a child — by the Holy Spirit.

19. Joseph, her husband, was a man who did what was right. He had no desire to disgrace her publicly, so he resolved to end the marriage quietly.

20. But while he was wrestling with this, an angel of the Lord appeared to him in a dream and said: "Joseph, son of David — do not be afraid to take Mary as your wife. What has been conceived in her is from the Holy Spirit.

21. She will bear a son, and you are to name him Jesus — for he will save his people from their sins."

22. All of this took place so that what the Lord had spoken through the prophet would be fulfilled:

23. *"Look — the virgin will conceive and bear a son, and they will call his name Immanuel"* — which means, God is with us.

24. When Joseph woke from sleep, he did what the angel of the Lord had commanded him: he took Mary as his wife.

25. But he did not know her intimately until she had given birth to a son. And he named him Jesus.

---
## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

### On the genealogy (vv. 1-17):

**Verse 1 — "record of the origin" for βίβλος γενέσεως:** The Sinaiticus opens Matthew with ΒΙΒΛΟΣ ΓΕΝΕΣΕΩΣ at the top of the first column. The phrase echoes Genesis 2:4 and 5:1 in the Septuagint — a deliberate literary signal. Matthew is announcing not merely a family tree but a new Genesis, a new beginning of God's work. I render it "record of the origin" rather than "book of the genealogy" because βίβλος here does not mean a physical book, and γενέσεως carries connotations of origin, becoming, and genesis — not merely a list of ancestors.

**Verse 1 — "Messiah" for Χριστοῦ:** Throughout this chapter I render Χριστός as "Messiah" rather than "Christ." In the 1st century, Χριστός was not a surname — it was a title, the Greek translation of the Hebrew מָשִׁיחַ (anointed one). English readers who hear "Jesus Christ" as a first-and-last name miss the force of what Matthew is claiming from his very first line: this Jesus is the long-awaited Anointed King.

**Verse 6 — the line break after "David the king":** David is the pivot of the entire genealogy. Matthew signals this by calling him ὁ βασιλεύς (the king) — a title given to no one else in the list. I set this on its own line. The Greek has no such break, but the rhetorical weight is real. David is the hinge — everything before points toward him; everything after descends from him toward exile and, finally, toward the Messiah.

**Verses 3, 5, 6 — the four women:** Matthew's inclusion of Tamar, Rahab, Ruth, and "the wife of Uriah" in a patrilineal genealogy is striking. Each is an outsider or figure of scandal. Matthew is preparing the reader for Mary: another woman whose pregnancy will look scandalous from the outside. The Sinaiticus identifies Bathsheba only as τῆς τοῦ Οὐρίου — "the [wife] of Uriah," never by her own name. The text's refusal to name her points the finger at David's sin rather than at the woman.

**Verses 7, 10 — "Asaph" and "Amos":** The Sinaiticus reads ΑΣΑΦ where later manuscripts read Asa, and ΑΜΩΣ where later manuscripts read Amon. Asaph is the name of the great psalmist; Amos the prophet. Whether deliberate or variant spellings, I follow the manuscript as it stands.

**Verse 16 — the passive construction:** The genealogy uses the active ἐγέννησεν ("fathered") forty times, then suddenly switches to the passive ἐγεννήθη ("was born") at the climactic moment. Joseph does not "father" Jesus. Jesus "was born" from Mary. The grammatical shift is the theological claim.

**Verse 17 — "So then":** The Greek οὖν carries summation and satisfaction — the author steps back to survey the architecture he has built. "So then" captures the rhetorical gesture: *Look at this structure. See how it all fits.*

### On the birth narrative (vv. 18-25):

**Verse 18 — "happened like this":** A dynamic rendering of the Greek construction signaling: now I will tell you how. "Was as follows" is stiff; "happened like this" has the energy of a storyteller leaning in.

**Verse 18 — "pledged in marriage":** Jewish betrothal (erusin) was legally binding — more than modern engagement. Dissolution required formal divorce. "Pledged in marriage" conveys the legal weight without a footnote.

**Verse 19 — "a man who did what was right" for δίκαιος ὤν:** The word δίκαιος means righteous, just, upright. But "righteous" in modern English often sounds self-righteous. Joseph's righteousness drives him to protect Mary from shame even while he believes she has betrayed him. "A man who did what was right" captures the active, compassionate quality of his justice.

**Verse 19 — "disgrace her publicly" for δειγματίσαι:** The Sinaiticus reads the simpler δειγματίσαι rather than the later variant παραδειγματίσαι. Both carry the sense of making a public example of someone.

**Verse 20 — "wrestling with this":** The Greek ἐνθυμηθέντος means pondering, deliberating. But the context makes clear this is not calm reflection — Joseph is in anguish. "Wrestling" captures the emotional weight.

**Verse 21 — the em dash before "for he will save":** The name Ἰησοῦς (Jesus) is the Greek form of the Hebrew יְהוֹשֻׁעַ/יֵשׁוּעַ, meaning "YHWH saves." The angel's explanation is a direct wordplay on the name. The name *is* the mission.

**Verse 23 — "Look" for ἰδού:** The Greek ἰδού demands attention. "Behold" is archaic; "see" is too weak. "Look" is direct and immediate. The Sinaiticus preserves the LXX's παρθένος (virgin) for the Hebrew עַלְמָה of Isaiah 7:14.

**Verse 25 — "did not know her intimately":** The Greek γινώσκω, like the Hebrew ידע, is used euphemistically for sexual relations. "Did not know her intimately" makes the meaning clear. The Sinaiticus omits "firstborn" (πρωτότοκον), reading simply "a son" — a shorter, likely earlier reading.

### On the Sinaiticus manuscript:

The two manuscript pages show Matthew 1 running across four columns of continuous uncial script in scriptio continua — no word breaks, no punctuation. The heading reads ΚΑΤΑ ΜΑΘΘΑΙΟΝ. What is visible even in the facsimile is the relentlessness of the genealogy: the ΔΕ ΕΓΕΝΝΗΣΕΝ refrain hammers forward name after name, building an almost hypnotic momentum. Then at verse 16, the pattern shatters. The grammar changes. The passive voice arrives. A good genealogy builds a rhythm so that the break in the rhythm *means something*.

— PNEUMA, AI Translator
